stimulus: Zora Neale Hurston's novel Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) is among the first major novels by a Black woman to draw extensively on Southern folk dialect. The book tells the story of Janie, a Black woman in Florida who searches for love and self-knowledge across three marriages. Janie's story reflects the experiences of many Black women in the rural South in the early twentieth century. Their Eyes Were Watching God thus shows how Hurston used fiction to portray the inner lives of Black Southern women, which she continued to do throughout her career.
stem: Which choice best states the main purpose of the text?

answerOptions.content: To claim that several characters in Their Eyes Were Watching God were based on people Hurston knew personally
To illustrate how the subjects Hurston addressed in her writing changed over the course of her career
To show how Hurston's novel inspired other Black women to write fiction in the 1930s
To give an example of how Hurston portrayed the experiences of Black Southern women in her fiction

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer. The text describes how Their Eyes Were Watching God depicted the life of a Black Southern woman and states that Hurston continued to portray such experiences throughout her career.

Choice A is incorrect. The text never mentions inspirations for any characters. Choice B is incorrect. The text never describes changes in Hurston's subject matter. Choice C is incorrect. The text never mentions other Black women writers.
